title: Education Cultures
summary: Builds the conditions for educators and educational innovation to thrive, demonstrate impact and shape the sector.
detailSummary:
Education Cultures will make King’s a place where educators are better able to lead, improve and influence education. Colleagues will have clearer opportunities to develop ideas, build confidence, access support, demonstrate impact and gain recognition for their contribution. Strong practice will be easier to surface and spread, while educational leadership and innovation will carry greater institutional esteem and external credibility.

For students, this should mean more consistently thoughtful, inclusive and evidence-informed educational experiences, shaped by educators who have the autonomy and support to improve practice. Over time, King’s will become better at learning from its own educational work, directing attention and investment towards what makes the greatest difference, and contributing a clearer and more credible voice to wider debates about education.
transformationClaim: King's has a connected, evidence-led education culture in which educators and students can shape strategic priorities, develop and test practice, demonstrate impact, gain recognition and extend King's influence.

deliverables.title: Education Culture Infrastructure
deliverables.summary: Creates the central and faculty-level infrastructure needed to strengthen education culture consistently across King’s.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Establishes the institutional infrastructure through which education culture can be convened, supported and connected more consistently across King’s. King’s Academy would provide central coordination, helping to establish shared direction, connect activity across faculties and services, support institutional conversations and ensure that educational development is linked to wider strategic priorities.

Alongside this, the deliverable would establish a proportionate expectation that every faculty has recognisable education-development capacity to support the Vice Dean Education and local educational leadership. The model would respond to faculty context rather than require identical structures everywhere. In some faculties this may build on an existing education-development unit. Elsewhere it may involve dedicated coordination or administrative support, supplemented where necessary by centrally held strategic project capacity.

The aim is to give every faculty a practical route for convening its education community, supporting colleagues to develop and share practice, connecting local priorities to institutional opportunities and ensuring that strong educational work is not dependent on informal relationships or individual goodwill. The deliverable would also clarify the respective roles of King’s Academy, faculties, professional services and institutional governance, creating a connected operating model without centralising ownership of educational practice.

deliverables.title: Strategic Communities of Practice
deliverables.summary: Creates resourced and influential communities around strategic educational priorities, giving distributed educational leadership a visible home and enabling collective expertise, innovation and evidence to shape shared institutional practice.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Strategic Communities of Practice are institutionally recognised communities that sit between strategy and educational practice. Convened around selected priorities, which might initially include areas such as experiential learning, AI in education or inclusive education, they bring together academic and professional service educators, students and partners to develop shared understanding, connect ground-up innovation and co-own educational change. They provide a locus of energy and influence for educational leadership, while helping King’s develop greater collective resonance without imposing uniform solutions from the centre.

Each community combines a committed core with a wider, porous periphery, while agile shards undertake focused work around particular challenges or opportunities. Through events, collaborative inquiry, commissioned activity, seed funding, showcases and wider outreach, communities engage colleagues beyond existing enthusiasts, test and evidence emerging ideas, and help effective practice travel across the institution. Workloaded conveners sustain the community, while an Innovation Subcommittee provides strategic direction, a commissioning budget and light-touch accountability. Together, this creates a practical mechanism through which distinctive King’s provisions, practices and pedagogies can emerge from distributed expertise and genuine co-construction.

caseForChange.problem:
King’s has substantial educational expertise, leadership and innovation distributed across faculties, professional services and student communities. Much of this work is thoughtful and ambitious, but it can remain locally bounded, weakly connected to related activity elsewhere and only loosely linked to institutional priorities. Promising ideas are therefore harder to discover, similar challenges may be addressed repeatedly, and opportunities for collective learning and wider influence are missed.

King’s also faces a challenge of coherence. The university increasingly needs to identify common points of resonance across its educational provision while preserving disciplinary difference, local agency and professional judgement. Central policy and standardisation can create consistency, but they can also become overly directive and generate compliance rather than shared ownership. King’s currently lacks a sufficiently influential middle layer through which common approaches can be developed collaboratively from practice.
caseForChange.opportunity:
Strategic Communities of Practice offer King’s a mechanism for distributed educational leadership. They create recognised spaces in which educators, students, professional services and partners can interpret shared challenges, connect local expertise and shape institutional responses together. In doing so, they provide a more visible and resourced home for educational leadership and a stronger route through which practice can inform strategy.

Sitting between strategy and practice, the communities can identify areas of common resonance, surface and connect promising work, convene sustained educational conversations, stimulate innovation and generate evidence for wider development. They also provide a way to steward investment more coherently, using light-touch governance and commissioning resource to support activity that has both local value and wider institutional relevance.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is renewing its focus on education as a field of academic leadership and on the development of credible, rewarding education-focused career pathways. This creates an opportunity to give colleagues a stronger locus for educational contribution, identity and influence, while connecting individual development with wider institutional improvement. It also creates a timely context for strengthening the visibility and esteem of educational leadership across the university.

At the same time, the university is seeking to add more deliberate value to educational practice in ways that enhance student outcomes and experiences. The Education Delivery Framework establishes a number of common themes around which provision across King’s can find greater collective resonance. These themes now need spaces in which they can be interpreted, challenged and developed by the people who teach, support and learn at King’s, rather than translated too quickly into centrally determined solutions.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s develops a repeatable model through which strategic educational priorities are shaped by distributed leadership and collective expertise. Colleagues gain visible, resourced and influential spaces in which to develop their practice, contribute beyond their immediate setting and shape wider educational direction. Ground-up innovation becomes easier to identify, connect, support and learn from, while institutional investment is more coherently stewarded.

The Education Delivery Framework becomes a basis for shared inquiry and development rather than a mechanism for uniformity. Common provisions, practices and pedagogies can emerge where they add value, while remaining responsive to disciplinary context, local expertise and student experience. Over time, King’s becomes better able to combine institutional coherence with local agency, so that educational leadership gains greater visibility and esteem, promising practice travels more readily, and educational change is more genuinely shared, evidence-informed and enduring.

deliverables.title: Educational Impact, Evidence and Recognition
deliverables.summary: Builds a shared approach to evidencing educational impact and using that evidence to strengthen recognition, progression and esteem.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Creates a shared institutional approach to understanding the impact of educational work on students and the health of education culture at King’s. It would bring together measures that already exist across the institution into a more coherent evaluation framework, helping colleagues ask what works, for whom, under what conditions and with what effect on student experience and outcomes.

The primary focus would be student impact, including satisfaction, academic achievement, career readiness, destinations, belonging, self-efficacy, wellbeing and engagement. Attendance and KEATS activity would provide important measures of institutional engagement, alongside broader participation in learning, services, enrichment and enhancement. Equity of impact would run through the framework, ensuring that aggregate improvement does not conceal uneven experience or outcomes between student groups.

The framework would be proportionate to the scale of the work being evaluated. Small exploratory interventions should be able to use light-touch methods, while larger institutional initiatives should be supported to develop stronger evaluation designs, baselines and comparative evidence. A central education impact and evaluation capability would help educators and projects use institutional data responsibly, develop theories of impact and make informed decisions about what should grow, change or stop.

Alongside student impact, the deliverable would establish a light-touch account of education culture itself. This could include patterns in education-focused promotion, participation in development opportunities and strategic communities, applications for innovation support, educational leadership activity and external engagement.

This stronger evidence base would also support recognition. Educators would have clearer ways to evidence their contribution and impact for progression, promotion and institutional recognition. This would include colleagues on academic education pathways and professional services educators whose directly educative work is often significant but less consistently visible. The deliverable would help turn educational contribution into credible narratives of leadership, impact and esteem, supported by practical opportunities such as recognised leadership roles, project secondments, bought-out time and development support.

deliverables.title: Innovation Incubation and External Influence
deliverables.summary: Creates a supported route through which promising educational ideas can be tested, developed, scaled and projected externally.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Establishes a coherent incubation model for educational innovation, giving promising ideas access to the time, resource, expertise and support needed to move from early exploration into credible practice. It would provide a visible route through which ideas emerging from faculties, services, Strategic Communities of Practice and student collaboration can be identified, tested, evaluated and developed.

The model would operate at more than one level. Small seed investments would support rapid experimentation, exploratory work and ideas that are not yet ready for substantial institutional commitment. A smaller number of strategic flagship interventions would receive more sustained support, stronger evaluation and clearer routes to institutional adoption or external profile. The approach would be open to innovation within the curriculum and in more agile co-curricular or extracurricular settings, including King’s Edge where appropriate.

Incubation would involve more than funding. It would connect innovators to learning design, project management, evaluation, data, student co-creation, partnership development and institutional decision routes. Promising practice would be supported to clarify the problem it addresses, test its assumptions, understand its impact and determine whether it should be scaled, embedded, adapted or concluded. Existing innovation funds could gradually be connected through this common framework, making investment more visible, strategically aligned and consistently evaluated.

The deliverable would also create deliberate routes for externalisation. King’s Academy would work with educators, communities and projects to turn credible practice and evidence into sector-facing outputs, including articles, reports, blogs, podcasts, conference contributions, partnerships, events and public conversations. The Policy Institute would act as a standing partner, helping connect educational practice to policy thinking and translate evaluated institutional experience into sector and government-facing influence.

The ambition is for King’s to build an external voice grounded in implemented practice and credible evidence. Educational innovation would therefore contribute not only to internal improvement, but also to institutional reputation, sector leadership and wider policy influence.

title: Curriculum Framework and Review
summary: Provides the route through which King's Edge requirements are embedded in curriculum audit, review and enhancement.
edgeRole: Carries the curriculum audit and review mechanism that surfaces skills, experiential learning, graduate premium contribution and growth priorities.

title: Student Experience Hub
summary: Creates a coherent and personalised digital student experience by progressively integrating existing systems and platforms, strengthening the CRM capability that connects services behind them, and developing digital enhancements within a shared institutional environment.
detailSummary:
The Student Experience Hub will provide a single, evolving digital environment through which students can discover information and opportunities, access services, complete actions, seek support and follow progress. Rather than replacing all existing systems at once, it will progressively integrate their information and functionality into clearer student journeys, reducing reliance on disconnected portals and routes.

Strengthened CRM capability will connect the services behind this experience, providing shared context, knowledge, workflows and case management so that interactions can be coordinated across teams and channels. Students should receive more consistent support, greater visibility of progress and less need to repeat information, while staff gain clearer ownership and understanding of the student journey.

A multidisciplinary Digital Innovation Development Team will evolve the Hub iteratively by integrating priority systems, testing digital enhancements and maturing successful features for sustained institutional use. This will include meeting the demands generated by King’s Edge—such as opportunity discovery, personalised curation, planning, participation, evidence and recognition—within capabilities designed for reuse across the wider student experience.

Over time, the Hub will bring CRM investment, platform integration and digital innovation into one coherent product direction, enabling King’s to continuously improve a more personalised, intelligent and responsive student experience.

caseForChange.problem:
King’s students currently experience the university through a fragmented collection of systems, portals and service channels. Information, opportunities, administrative tasks and support are distributed according to institutional structures rather than organised around student needs. Students must work out where to go, navigate inconsistent interfaces and often move between several systems or teams to complete a single journey.

This fragmentation continues behind the student-facing experience. Information about enquiries and interactions is not consistently shared; knowledge, routing and workflows vary between services; and ownership of next steps may be unclear. Students can be redirected, lose visibility of progress or have to repeat their circumstances, while staff may lack the context needed to provide timely and coordinated support.

King’s also lacks a sufficiently coherent route for meeting new digital needs. Requirements are liable to be addressed through local system changes, project-specific portals or separate products, reproducing common functions and expanding the digital estate without making the student experience more joined up.
caseForChange.opportunity:
The Student Experience Hub creates the opportunity to bring the student-facing experience, the institutional service infrastructure behind it and the development of new digital capabilities into one evolving model.

The app can progressively integrate existing systems and platforms, surfacing relevant information and functionality through coherent student journeys rather than requiring students to navigate each underlying system separately. This allows King’s to build from what already exists, rationalise duplicated entry points over time and develop new capabilities only where current provision is absent or inadequate.

CRM investment can provide the shared context, knowledge, workflow and case-management capability through which King’s responds when students take action or seek support. It connects the visible digital experience with the services and teams responsible for fulfilling it, enabling clearer ownership, better routing and more consistent follow-through.

A Digital Innovation Development Team can then evolve the Hub continuously. Working within shared architecture, standards and governance, it can integrate services, prototype new journeys and develop digital enhancements directly within the common environment. Requirements generated by King’s Edge—including opportunity discovery, personalised curation, planning, participation, evidence and recognition—can be met through this model, with the underlying capabilities designed for wider reuse rather than enclosed within a separate Edge product.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is at an inflection point.

Student frustration with fragmented systems, poor communication and difficult service journeys is already pronounced. Existing strategic work identifies both a unified digital front door and improved CRM-enabled enquiry management as high institutional priorities, reflecting the limits of continued local or incremental fixes.

At the same time, significant decisions are approaching about CRM investment, system integration and the future student app. King’s Edge is also moving into delivery and will generate substantial new demand for digital functionality, while early portal work has begun to establish elements of the architecture and development culture needed for in-house development.

These pressures are converging. Unless they are aligned before investment and development accelerate, King’s risks committing to separate CRM, app and programme-specific solutions that reproduce the fragmentation it is trying to resolve. The full end state will necessarily be delivered in phases, but the shared direction, ownership and architectural foundations need to be established now.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s moves from a fragmented collection of student-facing systems and service channels to a single, continuously evolving digital relationship with students.

The Student Experience Hub becomes the primary environment through which students discover information and opportunities, complete actions, access services and seek support. Existing platforms remain authoritative where appropriate, but their information and functionality are progressively integrated into coherent journeys, reducing duplicated entry points and the need for students to understand internal organisational structures.

Behind the Hub, strengthened CRM, knowledge and workflow capabilities connect the services responsible for fulfilling those journeys. Staff work from clearer shared context, enquiries have visible ownership and progress, and students are less likely to be redirected, lose track of an issue or repeat their circumstances across multiple teams.

New digital enhancements are developed within this same environment rather than through additional standalone products. A multidisciplinary development team integrates systems, tests new ideas and matures successful prototypes into reliable institutional capabilities. This includes delivering the feature requirements generated by King’s Edge while designing those capabilities for wider use across the student experience.

The result is a reinforcing model in which CRM investment, system integration and digital innovation contribute to one coherent product direction. Each new development improves the shared student experience rather than adding another layer to the digital estate.

edgeRole: Provides the shared digital environment through which King’s Edge feature needs—including opportunity discovery, personalised curation, planning, participation, evidence and recognition—are delivered as reusable capabilities within the wider student experience.
